data.title: 'I'm Worth $7.3 Million,' Physician Says — Yet A $3 Drink For His Kids Gives Him Pause As 'Ramsey Show' Host Urges Him To 'Loosen The Purse Strings'
data.description:
He has built significant wealth, yet a simple drink at dinner still makes his heart race. 

Mark told "The Ramsey Show" that despite earning about $390,000 a year as a physician in Honolulu, he feels anxious whenever his family spends even a few extra dollars. "I'm worth $7.3 million," he said, yet small purchases still unsettle him.

"Man, that extra $3 we could have saved," he said of his reaction when his children ask for an extra drink at dinner. Mark asked hosts John Delony and Jade Warshaw whether he can afford to go part-time.

Don't Miss:

A Childhood That Wired Him For Caution

"You've chosen not to," Delony said, interrupting Mark after he said he "can't" approve extra spending.

Mark said he grew up watching his parents move from a one-bedroom apartment into a more stable life through disciplined saving. He said money felt uncertain during his childhood.

"Mark, give yourself some grace," Delony said. "Your nervous system is wired up this way." He said growing up around financial instability can shape how someone reacts to money later in life. 

Can He Step Back From Work?

Mark said he owns rental properties valued at about $2 million, all paid off, generating roughly $8,000 a month in net income. 

Trending: This ETF issuer isn't chasing the index — it's building tools for income, leverage, and conviction

"I don't see any money issues here show me where the money problem is," Warshaw said after reviewing his income and assets.

He paid off his medical school loans in his first year out of training, has practiced medicine for 20 years and feels "so wound up" about money that it spills into his marriage.

Delony said that as a physician who spends his days solving problems and facing uncertainty, Mark may be holding tightly to the one thing he can control: his money. Warshaw said the numbers did not show a financial problem and told him he simply needs to "loosen the purse strings."

"Go part time, homie," Delony said, after Mark explained he wanted to scale back to spend more time at home.

Cutting Back Without Falling Behind

Mark said reducing his schedule to three days a week would lower his annual income from about $390,000 to $220,000. He said living in Hawaii means high state taxes, which adds to his hesitation.

Warshaw said that if he carries any debt on the rental properties, he should pay it off and simplify his financial life.

"Do it. You’re all good. Do it," Warshaw said.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: What's Driving the Market Sentiment Around ChowChow Cloud International Holdings Ltd?
data.description:
ChowChow Cloud International Holdings Ltd's (NYSE:CHOW) short interest as a percent of float has fallen 35.33% since its last report. According to exchange reported data, there are now 344 thousand shares sold short, which is 3.24% of all regular shares that are available for trading. Based on its trading volume, it would take traders 1.41 days to cover their short positions on average.

Why Short Interest Matters

Short interest is important to track because it can act as an indicator of market sentiment towards a particular stock. An increase in short interest can signal that investors have become more bearish, while a decrease in short interest can signal they have become more bullish.

ChowChow Cloud International Holdings Ltd Short Interest Graph (3 Months)

As you can see from the chart above the percentage of shares that are sold short for ChowChow Cloud International Holdings Ltd has declined since its last report. This does not mean that the stock is going to rise in the near-term but traders should be aware that less shares are being shorted.

Comparing ChowChow Cloud International Holdings Ltd's Short Interest Against Its Peers

Peer comparison is a popular technique amongst analysts and investors for gauging how well a company is performing. A company's peer is another company that has similar characteristics to it, such as industry, size, age, and financial structure. You can find a company's peer group by reading its 10-K, proxy filing, or by doing your own similarity analysis.

According to Benzinga Pro, ChowChow Cloud International Holdings Ltd's peer group average for short interest as a percentage of float is 3.57%, which means the company has less short interest than most of its peers.

This article was generated by Benzinga's automated content engine and was reviewed by an editor.

data.title: 'Running A Small Business Is Exhausting,' Says A Coffee Shop Owner. 'I Used To Like Coffee. Now It Just Haunts My Dreams'
data.description:
Running a small coffee shop was supposed to be about community, good conversations and, of course, great coffee. Instead, one owner who has been running a small shop for a couple of years says it has turned into a nonstop grind of stress, repairs and paperwork.

“I used to like coffee,” the owner wrote in a recent Reddit post. “Now it just haunts my dreams.”

The Messy Middle Of Small Business

The breaking point that day was an espresso machine that “decided to give up on life” right in the middle of the morning rush. Customers were waiting. Orders were piling up. Repair calls had to be made. And the complaints started rolling in.

Don't Miss:

  • The ‘ChatGPT of Marketing' Just Opened a $0.85/Share Round — 10,000+ Investors Are Already In
  • It’s no wonder Jeff Bezos holds over $250 million in art — this alternative asset has outpaced the S&P 500 since 1995, delivering an average annual return of 11.4%. Here’s how everyday investors are getting started.

“Running a small business is exhausting,” the owner said. “Spent the whole day calling in repair guys and dealing with cranky customers who just want their caffeine fix.”

But the espresso machine was just the latest problem in what the owner described as a “constant juggling act” that never seems to end. Staffing. Inventory. Marketing. Compliance. And then, always, paperwork.

“Why is there always so much paperwork?” the owner asked. “Do they really need to know every single bean I buy?”

That frustration struck a chord with other small business owners. “We don’t own a small business, it owns us,” one replied.  Another said people constantly tell them how lucky they are not to have a boss. “I LOL every time,” they wrote.

Several owners pointed out that customers, employees, vendors and regulators all become bosses in their own way. “We are here to not have a boss, which actually turns out we have about 1,000 small bosses,” one commenter joked.

Trending: Disney Was Built on Character IP — This Pre-IPO Company Is Using the Same Playbook

Many described years two and three as the hardest stretch. Big enough to be complicated, but not organized enough to feel stable. “Year 2 was utter hell,” one bakery owner wrote. “Year 3 was just regular hell.”

Equipment failures were a common theme. “Everything and I mean everything starts to break in the 3-5 year range,” said an alcohol business owner with $1.3 million in annual sales. Once warranties expire, the capital expenses “kick in the teeth,” they added.

Death By A Thousand Small Problems

Others emphasized that the real exhaustion doesn’t come from one catastrophic event. It comes from constant small interruptions. A staff member calls in sick. A supplier runs out of stock. A permit needs renewing. Freight costs rise. Something leaks. Something breaks.

“Small businesses aren’t exhausting because of one big problem,” one commenter wrote. “They’re exhausting because of constant small interruptions.”

The mental load, many agreed, never switches off. Even when the doors close, the responsibility remains.

Still, not everyone would trade it for a steady office job. “If I knew it was gonna be like this I wouldn’t have started,” one service business owner admitted. “However, I cannot imagine going back to an office job.”

Several owners said the turning point came when they stopped trying to do everything themselves. “Systematize everything so if something happens your staff know what to do,” one wrote. “Delegate trust,”  Another advised creating simple checklists for “what to do when X breaks.”

Check out their venture portfolio today. Start investing in minutes and with as little as $10.

As for the coffee shop owner, the question remains whether the stress is temporary growing pains or a signal to make changes. “Sometimes I wonder if I should just pack it up and find a nice quiet office job,” they wrote.

For now, at least, they are still brewing.

Image: Shutterstock
